does: This paper benchmarks the performance of large language models as academic peer reviewers across twelve different models on 898 papers from NeurIPS and ICLR. The evaluation measures three key dimensions: rating calibration and whether LLMs align with human reviewer judgments, divergence in review emphasis and content patterns, and robustness against adversarial attacks. The authors find that LLMs systematically overrate weaker papers, emphasize reproducibility concerns while underemphasizing clarity issues compared to humans, produce substantially longer reviews with reduced vocabulary diversity, and remain highly vulnerable to prompt injection attacks embedded through invisible font-mapping techniques. They demonstrate that hidden instructions can artificially elevate low-scoring papers to acceptance-level ratings with varying effectiveness across model families.
connects: This work extends growing literature examining LLMs in high-stakes evaluation contexts and builds on earlier research into LLM reliability and alignment with human judgment. It connects to studies of bias and calibration in automated scoring systems, prior work on prompt injection vulnerabilities and adversarial robustness in language models, and research documenting systematic divergences between LLM outputs and human preferences across various tasks. The paper also relates to broader investigations of how neural models develop spurious correlations and fail to match human reasoning patterns, particularly in understanding what features LLMs weight differently than humans. It combines concerns from multiple strands: AI safety research on model robustness, fairness literature on algorithmic bias, and recent studies on using LLMs for review automation in scientific workflows.
enables: This work establishes a rigorous benchmark framework for evaluating LLMs in institutional review contexts and points toward necessary safeguards before broader deployment in peer review systems. It enables future research on prompt injection defenses and adversarial robustness specific to long-form review generation. The findings motivate development of calibration techniques that align LLM ratings with human standards, particularly for identifying weaker submissions accurately. The work enables investigation of whether domain-specific fine-tuning or retrieval-augmented approaches could improve topical judgment and reduce standardization. It also sets up research on interpretability methods to understand which paper features LLMs attend to versus what human reviewers prioritize. Ultimately, it provides concrete benchmarks against which improved LLM reviewer designs could be measured, supporting the eventual safe integration of automated review assistance into peer evaluation while maintaining system integrity.
